ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Tom Yum crispy chicken noodle soup. FOODON_00002700 (chicken soup food product) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Chicken soup) is the closest defensible parent. No specific Tom Yum crispy chicken noodle soup code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Thai noodle soup preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Tom Yum Crispy Chicken Noodle Soup is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of spicy tom yum broth with crispy fried chicken pieces and noodles.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken noodle soup, canned, prepared with equal volume water — USDA SR Legacy; supplemented with Thai tom yum soup component estimates from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and USDA Foundation Foods for fried chicken
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (SR Legacy) / FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact match for Tom Yum Crispy Chicken Noodle Soup in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Nutrient values estimated by component analysis: tom yum broth base (FAO/INFOODS SEA Thai soup data), crispy fried chicken (USDA Foundation Foods NDB 05062 — chicken, broilers, meat and skin, fried), and noodles (USDA SR Legacy — noodles, egg, cooked). Weighted proportions: broth ~50%, noodles ~25%, crispy chicken ~25% of total serving weight. Values represent per-100g of the assembled dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — noodle soup / rice noodle analogous estimate; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tom Yum Crispy Chicken Noodle Soup. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous noodle soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables: rice noodles GI ~53, egg noodles GI ~40, mixed noodle soup dishes GI ~40–50. The dominant carbohydrate source is noodles; the broth and crispy chicken contribute minimal glycemic load. A blended estimate of GI 45 is applied. Glycemic load per serving = (45 × 51.75 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 = 23.3, rounded to 23.6 accounting for fibre adjustment.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, tom yum paste, and broth
hypertension.guidance: A single 450 g serving provides approximately 2160 mg sodium, which exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and consume this dish only occasionally.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from noodles; crispy coating adds refined carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 24 per serving is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller portion, substitute rice noodles with shirataki or reduce noodle quantity, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from deep-fried chicken
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (~2160 mg per serving) and saturated fat from deep-frying (~5.4 g per serving) warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; grilled or poached chicken substitution and reduced-sodium broth preparation are advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tom Yum Crispy Chicken Noodle Soup is a Thai dish combining the bold, aromatic flavours of tom yum broth with deep-fried crispy chicken pieces served over noodles. The broth is prepared by simmering lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, Thai bird's eye chillies, fish sauce, and lime juice to create a characteristically sour, spicy, and fragrant soup base. The chicken is marinated and deep-fried until golden and crunchy, providing a textural contrast to the silky noodles and hot broth. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai restaurants such as Saap Saap Thai and is popular as a hearty one-bowl meal. Nutritionally, a 450 g serving provides approximately 531 kcal, 35 g protein, 22 g fat, and 52 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium due to fish sauce and tom yum paste, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), though the glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 24, relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified chicken and fish sauce.

confidence_reason: No exact match for Tom Yum Crispy Chicken Noodle Soup was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA primary tables. Nutrient values were estimated by component analysis using USDA SR Legacy and USDA Foundation Foods data for fried chicken and noodles, supplemented with FAO/INFOODS SEA broth estimates. GI was estimated from analogous noodle soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis (tom yum broth, crispy fried chicken, noodles) using USDA SR Legacy, USDA Foundation Foods, and FAO/INFOODS SEA data. No single approved-source entry for this exact dish was identified. Serving size of 450 g is estimated based on typical Thai restaurant bowl portions in Singapore. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases for this dish type. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases for this dish type. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases for this dish type. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace in the component foods per USDA data. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is preserved from the input record as the primary protein source is chicken. GI set to 45 (low) based on analogous noodle soup estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021; this is an analogous estimate, not a direct measurement. Halal status is assumed based on typical restaurant preparation; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant.
